title: 6 2 Special Matrices
body:
6.2 SPECIAL MATRICES


Inverse of a product: (AB)Рѕњ1 = B Рѕњ1 AРѕњ1 . Transpose of a product: (AB)T = B T AT .

A matrix Q is said to be orthogonal if

                                       QT Q = QQT = I

That is, the transpose of an orthogonal matrix is its inverse, QРѕњ1 = QT .

The columns of an orthogonal matrix are vectors that form an orthonormal set.

If A = AT is a real symmetric matrix then:

    Рђб All eigenvalues of A are real.

    Рђб There is always a full set of eigenvectors.

    Рђб Eigenvectors corresponding to different eigenvalues are orthogonal.

    Рђб If Q is the matrix of unit eigenvectors of A then Q is an orthogonal matrix and the
      diagonalisation of A is
                                           QT AQ = D
      .


In this lecture we will look at some important properties of invertible matrices, orthogonal
matrices, and symmetric matrices.


Example 1 Prove that for any square invertible matrices A and B of the same dimension

                                    (AB)Рѕњ1 = B Рѕњ1 AРѕњ1

                                           2
 An orthonormal set is a set of vectors that are (i) perpendicular to each other and (ii)
have unit length. For example, if u, v and w are an orthonormal set then

             u ┬и v = uT v = 0,    v ┬и w = vT w = 0,       u ┬и w = uT w = 0.

             u ┬и u = uT u = 1,    v ┬и v = vT v = 1,       w ┬и w = wT w = 1.
Note that we have used the fact that dot products can always be expressed as matrix
products. For example

                                           3
 An orthogonal matrix Q is a matrix whose columns form an orthonormal set of vectors.
An efficient way of saying this is that QT Q = I since all the dot products of the columns
are 0 when the columns are different and 1 when they are the same. This means that
an orthogonal matrix has the special property that its inverse is simply its transpose:
QT = QРѕњ1 .
                              1 Рѕњ2        1

                         №Бг 1
                         №Бг Рѕџ             Рѕњ5   №Би

 Example 6 Prove that the determinant of a real orthogonal matrix Q is ┬▒1

A useful way to think about orthogonal matrices is that they represent rotations. For
example, the matrix                                
                                     cos ╬И Рѕњ sin ╬И
                              Q=
                                     sin ╬И cos ╬И
is an orthogonal matrix that rotates a vector counter-clockwise by an angle ╬И. We can
see this by multiplying Q by the unit vectors in x and y-directions
                                                              
          cos ╬И Рѕњ sin ╬И     1                       cos ╬И Рѕњ sin ╬И    0
                                 =                                       =
          sin ╬И cos ╬И       0                       sin ╬И cos ╬И      1




Because it is an orthogonal matrix, the transpose of the rotation matrix is also its inverse,
that is, rotation by an angle Рѕњ╬И.
                                                           
                              T      Рѕњ1       cos ╬И sin ╬И
                             Q =Q =                           .
                                             Рѕњ sin ╬И cos ╬И




                                             5
                        Eigenvectors of Symmetric Matrices

When A is a real symmetric matrix, the process of diagonalisation simplifies considerably.

Theorem: Let A be a real symmetric matrix. Then

I) All eigenvalues of A are real.

II) There is always a full set of eigenvectors.

III) Eigenvectors corresponding to different eigenvalues are orthogonal.

The proofs of these statements are in the additional course notes. Property (III) is
especially significant. It means that the eigenvectors of A form an orthogonal basis set.
If in addition we make the eigenvectors have magnitude one then the eigenvectors will
form an orthonormal set and the matrix of eigenvectors Q is an orthogonal matrix. This
means that the diagonalisation procedure for a symmetric matrix A = AT is

                                       QT AQ = D

where QT = QРѕњ1 is an orthogonal matrix.

Example 7 Suppose that A, B and C are square matrices of the same dimension and
that A is symmetric and B is orthogonal. Simplify (ABC)T (B Рѕњ1 A)Рѕњ1 .




                                                                           F CT      F
